coredata.dc:title: Bottom-up synthesis and structural design strategy for graphene quantum dots with tunable emission to the near infrared region
coredata.dc:creator: Huang, Dapeng
Zhou, Haifeng
Wu, Yaqiang
Wang, Tao
Sun, Leilei
Gao, Peng
Sun, Yuzhen
Huang, Huining
Zhou, Guangjun
Hu, Jifan
coredata.dc:description: Despite recent advances in the fabrication of graphene quantum dots (GQDs) with excellent fluorescence performance, it has been challenging to extend the fluorescence emission to deep red and short wave near-infrared. Herein, we present a strategy to reach the goal via hydrothermal treatment of polythiophene derivatives which mainly comprises a polythiophene conjugate skeleton, lots of benzene ring structure and alkyl chain. This structure is thermally converted into a doped crystalline GQDs at 170 °C for 20 h with the maximum fluorescence emission at 700 nm. In addition, the length of alkyl chain also has a regulatory effect on emission wavelength of final products, which enables the chemical molecular-level structural design of GQDs with specific light emission waveband.

xocs:funding.xocs:funding-agency-matched-string: National Natural Science Foundations of China
National Science Foundation of Shandong Province
Shenzhen Science and Technology Research and Development Funds

xocs:funding-list.xocs:funding-text: This work was supported by National Science Foundation of Shandong Province ( ZR2016EMM20 , ZR2016EMQ09 ), Shenzhen Science and Technology Research and Development Funds ( JCYJ20160331173823401 ) and the National Natural Science Foundations of China ( 51472150 ). The authors deeply acknowledge Prof. Huaijin Zhang, Haohai Yu, Xiaoqiang Yu and Qi Fang for their support in this project. The authors thank Dr. Shouzhi Wang and Yingying Jin for assistance with cyclic voltammetry characterization.

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: (a) Normalized UV/Vis absorption spectra of PAT16, PAT16-120 °C-12 h, PAT16-150 °C-12 h, and PAT16-170 °C-12 h. (b) Normalized UV/Vis absorption spectra of PAT16-170 °C-8 h, PAT16-170 °C-12 h, PAT16-170 °C-16 h, and PAT16-170 °C-20 h. (c) Normalized PL spectra (300 nm excitation) of PAT16, PAT16-170 °C-8 h, PAT16-170 °C-12 h, PAT16-170 °C-16 h, and PAT16-170 °C −20 h. (d) Normalized absorption spectra, PL excitation spectra (700 nm emission) and PL spectra (300 nm excitation) of PAT16-170 °C-20 h (named as GQDs). The inserts show photographs of GQDs under illumination of daylight (left) and UV (365 nm) light (right). (A colour version of this figure can be viewed online.)

(a) The Raman spectra of products under 170 °C for 8 h, 12 h, 16 h, 20 h (b) The Raman spectra of GQDs derived from polythiophene derivatives with alkaneC12, alkaneC14, alkaneC16. (A colour version of this figure can be viewed online.)

(a) TEM of GQDs produced at 170 °C for 20 h. Inset: Size distribution of GQDs. (b) The electron diffraction pattern of the GQDs indicate d1 = 0.255 nm (in-plane diffractions) and d2 = 0.174 nm (004 facet) (c) High-resolution TEM image of individual GQDs and the lattice fringe is observed. (d) The line-profile analysis of the S-GQD as shown in (c) The lattice spacing is 0.25 nm. (A colour version of this figure can be viewed online.)
FT-IR spectra of (a) PAT16 (b) PAT16-170 °C-8 h (c) PAT16-170 °C-12 h (d) PAT16-170 °C-16 h (e) PAT16-170 °C-20 h. (A colour version of this figure can be viewed online.)

(a) The XRD pattern of the GQDs. (b) The full-scan XPS spectrum of the GQDs and high-resolution spectrum of C1s (c), N1s (d), O1s (e) and S2p (f) of the GQDs. (A colour version of this figure can be viewed online.)
Typical time-resolved fluorescence-decay curve of GQDs recorded at 700 nm with (a) Ex = 270 nm. (b) Ex = 300 nm. (c) Ex = 320 nm. (A colour version of this figure can be viewed online.)
The proposed fluorescent mechanism of N, S co-doped GQDs. (A colour version of this figure can be viewed online.)

A comparison of the photostabilities of the GQDs (checked at 700 nm) and CdSe/ZnS core-shell QDs (checked at 643 nm). The samples were continuously irradiated using a 450 W xenon lamp. I0 and I are the PL intensity of the samples before and after irradiation, respectively. After 60 min of irradiation, no obvious decline was observed in the PL of the GQDs. (A colour version of this figure can be viewed online.)

Confocal fluorescent images of living bone marrow-derived (C57BL/6 mice) dendritic cells (a, b, c) and human adipose-derived stem cells (d, e, f) incubated with 50 μg/mL GQDs. Incubation time: 30 min. Excitation wavelength: 473 nm; Detection wavelength: >650 nm. (A colour version of this figure can be viewed online.)
(a) Synthetic routes of model poly(3-alkylthiophene). (b) Different regioregularities: head-to-tail (HT), head-to-head (HH) and tail-to-tail (TT) linkages.
(a) The existence of what are essentially single bonds between the aromatic cycles allows the occurrence of interannular rotations. (b) The accumulation state of the polythiophene chains.
Main electrochemical data and Eg for products derived from different hydrothermal temperature in 12h.

head.ce:title: Bottom-up synthesis and structural design strategy for graphene quantum dots with tunable emission to the near infrared region

ce:affiliation.ce:label: a
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: State Key Laboratory of Crystal Materials, Shandong University, Jinan, 250100, PR China

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: State Key Laboratory of Crystal Materials

ce:affiliation.ce:label: b
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: School of Materials Science and Engineering, Qilu University of Technology (Shandong Academy of Sciences), Jinan, Shandong, 250353, PR China

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: School of Materials Science and Engineering
Qilu University of Technology (Shandong Academy of Sciences)

ce:affiliation.ce:label: c
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: Shenzhen Research Institute of Shandong University, Shenzhen, 518057, PR China

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: Shenzhen Research Institute of Shandong University

ce:correspondence.ce:text: Corresponding author. State Key Laboratory of Crystal Materials, Shandong University, Jinan, 250100, PR China.

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: State Key Laboratory of Crystal Materials

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: Despite recent advances in the fabrication of graphene quantum dots (GQDs) with excellent fluorescence performance, it has been challenging to extend the fluorescence emission to deep red and short wave near-infrared. Herein, we present a strategy to reach the goal via hydrothermal treatment of polythiophene derivatives which mainly comprises a polythiophene conjugate skeleton, lots of benzene ring structure and alkyl chain. This structure is thermally converted into a doped crystalline GQDs at 170 °C for 20 h with the maximum fluorescence emission at 700 nm. In addition, the length of alkyl chain also has a regulatory effect on emission wavelength of final products, which enables the chemical molecular-level structural design of GQDs with specific light emission waveband.

ce:section.ce:para: Many carbonaceous materials with various morphologies have been investigated, such as fullerene, carbon nanotubes, nanofibers, porous carbon, graphene and their hybrids. Among these materials, graphene sheets with several nanometer, were generally called as graphene quantum dots (GQDs). Because of the unique physical and chemical properties such as excellent fluorescence, easy surface functionalization, high resistance to photobleaching, low toxicity and good biocompatibility, the GQDs have drawn considerable attention in fields of biotechnology and medicine recently [1–3]. Generally, the preparation strategies of GQDs were classified as “top-down” and “bottom-up” ways. The top-down strategy is based on cleaving or breaking down of carbonaceous materials including graphite, graphene, carbon fibers, carbon nanotubes, graphene oxide, and carbon black via hydrothermal, strong acid oxidation and electrochemical exfoliation. The GQDs derived from top-down way almost all had fluorescence-emission in relatively short wavelength and lacked effective control on morphology and size distribution. As for the bottom-up way, it gives us more space to design products with desired performance. Bottom-up methods, relying on the chemical reaction from precursors or assembly of small building blocks, are convenient in controlling the morphologies and structures of nanomaterials. Citric acid [4], pyrene [5], glucose [6] and the mixture or derivatives are usually used to prepare GQDs by approaches, such as pyrolysis or hydrothermal treatment and microwave-assisted hydrothermal treatment. Xin Yan et al. used developed oxidative condensation reactions to synthesize stable colloidal graphene quantum dots with tunable size successfully [7]. Lately, carbon nanobelts with cylindrical shape have been synthesized through iterative Wittig reactions followed by a nickel-mediated aryl-aryl coupling reaction [8]. Moreno et al. have succeeded in preparing nanoporous graphene with atomic precision by bottom-up design of the molecular precursors [9]. These organic synthesis methods supported us for more controllability of the structure and morphology. And currently, many efforts on element doping have been carried out to change the electronic structure of GQDs and tune their optical and electrical properties [10–14]. But the element doping through bottom-up routes mainly focus on the selection of diversified organic precursors that contain desired heteroatoms and carbonization conditions. The thermal conversion process could bring undesired by-products and poorly controlled radical reactions. Hence, if doped atoms couldn't permeate to the carbon matrix effectively, the modulation for electronic structure and optical properties of GQDs would be limited. So turning the emission wavelength of GQDs to a longer wavelength remains a challenge. Up to now, there are few reports of carbon dots with long-wavelength emissions. For example, Mandal et al. reported efficient orange-emissive carbon dots with optimal emission at 590 nm [15]. Zhang and co-workers reported efficient red-emissive CNDs with optimal emission at 615 nm and an on–off fluorescent probe was prepared for detecting glutathione (GSH) based on aggregation-induced fluorescence quenching [16]. Yang and co-workers reported highly efficient red emissive nitrogen-doped carbonized polymer dots with optimal emission at around 630 nm [17]. Although carbon dots with long wavelength emissions have been obtained, their emission wavelengths in the deep red or NIR ranges still have space to explore. Recently, Wang et al. made pioneering use of polythiophene derivatives as carbon sources to synthesize GQDs with deep red emission and succeeded in applying GQDs to the fields of photodynamic therapy and photothermal therapy [18,19]. Tang et al. reported an approach to generate or to enhance the near-infrared luminescence from the green luminescent carbon nanodots. Experiments reveal that the near-infrared emission is significantly enhanced by thermally assisted growth in vacuum, which was attributed to the formation of the larger nano-domains from the small carbon clusters at elevated temperatures [20]. These works have made pioneering explorations in deep red and near-infrared light, but still need to explore in further realizing the tunability of long wave emission and the control of structure. The excellent performances of deep-red and near-infrared light prompt us to further control the evolution process from molecular-level and get the GQDs with controllable morphology and long wavelength emission.

Poly(3-alkylthiophenes) (P3ATs), an unusual class of polymers, have been widely studied for their good solubility, processibility, environmental stability, electroactivity, and other interesting properties [21–24]. P3ATs were achieved by introducing alkyl group into the β-position of the thiophene ring, whose bandgap Eg could be tuned by controlling the extent of the effective conjugation in the poly(thiophene) backbone [25]. So, the red shift of the absorption and emission spectra resulting from a decrease of Eg will reflect the enhancement of conjugate level.

Herein, we selected the P3ATs as precursor, aiming to base the conjugate thiophene skeleton while converting R moiety into an extended conjugate structure, to synthesize doped GQDs. Structure-preserving carbonization has been proved to be feasible by self-assembly of block-copolymers or surfactant templates [26–28]. Moreover, the conversion of organic precursor into ordered carbonaceous frameworks has also been realized [29]. So combining the two possible features of P3ATs that the structure-preserving for conjugate thiophene skeleton and the conversion into ordered carbonaceous frameworks for R moiety, it was worthy of our research on the structure evolution of P3ATs. Because its alkyl chains have very rich carbon yields in carbonization reactions, which contributes to extending the conjugated structure based on conjugate thiophene skeleton. In order to introduce nitrogen and simplify the assembly process, with the help of Suzuki reaction, the 3-substitution group of P3ATs was designed as the structure shown in the Scheme 1 . Wang et al. have successfully prepared GQDs with such precursors, and have studied its potential application in the biomedical field in detail [18,19]. But the evolution mechanism and the relationship between structure and performance of such precursor are still unclear. So In this work, we provide detailed examination on the relationship between the synthesis conditions (temperature, duration) and product properties, which is highly important for obtaining a good understanding of the reaction mechanism and optimal strategies. Besides, efforts have been made to provide a detailed account on the structural evolution of P3ATs during hydrothermal process and an in-depth insight on the structure–property relationship of GQDs. Rely on this structure, high water-dispersible GQDs were prepared through hydrothermal treatment and the fluorescence emission was extended to deep red and short wave near-infrared by adjusting length of alkyl side chains. The results suggest that the present method has certain feasibility in aspect of synthesizing desired carbonaceous materials by well-designed chemical structures of precursor.

4-Bromobenzyl bromide, N,N-dimethyldodecylamine, N,N-dimethyltetradecylamine, N,N-dimethylhexadecylamine, thiophene-3-boronic acid, tetrakis (triphenylphosphine) palladium(0) were purchased form Alfa Aesar. Other reagents were purchased from Beijing Chemical Regent Co. All reagents were of analytical grade and used as received without further purification. Noted, CHCl3 were distilled from MgSO4 to remove residual water. Milli-Qultrapurewater (18.2 MΩ) was used in experiment.

1H and 13C spectra were recorded on a Bruker Advance 400 spectrometer. HRMS spectra were recorded on a Q-TOF6510 spectrograph (Agilent). UV–vis absorption spectra were recorded on a Shimadzu UV-2600 spectrophotometer. Photoluminescence spectra were recorded on a Edinburgh Instruments (FLS980) fluorescence spectrophotometer. The PL quantum yields of aqueous GQDs were measured by an absolute method using the Edinburgh Instruments (FLS980) integrating sphere excited with Xe lamp. The fluorescence lifetime was measured on an F980 photo counting system (Edinburgh Instruments Ltd, UK). Elemental analyses of carbon, nitrogen, oxygen and sulfur were performed on a X-ray photo-electron spectroscopy(XPS) with a Thermo Fisher Scientific Escalab 250 spectrometer equipped with an Al Kα source, and C1s (284.6 eV) was used to calibrate the peak positions of various elements. The morphologies of prepared GQDs were characterized by a transmission electron microscopy at an acceleration voltage of 200 kV. (TEM, JEM 2100F, Japan). Atomic force microscopy (AFM) measurements were conducted using the DI Multimode SPM from Veeco Systems and the images were obtained with the tapping mode under ambient conditions, and samples were prepared by dropping the solution on the substrate of mica. Fourier transformation infrared (FTIR) spectra of the samples were recorded on a Thermo-Nicolet NEXUS 670 infrared spectrometer. X-ray diffraction (XRD) patterns were recorded on a Bruker-AXS D8 Advance X-ray diffractometer with Cu Kα radiation (λ = 1.54186 Å), and the scanning speed was 0.04 s/step. The ultraviolet photoelectron spectroscopy (UPS) measurements were carried out using a He I (21.22 eV) excitation line and a hemispherical analyzer (Leybold EA-11). Cyclic voltammetry were carried out on AMETEK Parstat 2273 instrument.

Poly(3-alkylthiophenes) (P3ATs) of different chain lengths (alkaneC12, alkaneC14, alkaneC16) were prepared according to a reported procedure [30]. The synthetic routes of model polymers P3ATs with alkaneC16 was outlined in Scheme 1 and the details were described below. The details of P3ATs with alkaneC12 and alkaneC14 were same to above, which were elided and only nuclear magnetic spectra were provided in supporting materials. The Suzuki coupling reaction was conducted under a nitrogen atmosphere and by avoiding light exposure.

4-Bromobenzyl bromide (0.25 g, 1 mmol) was dissolved in 20 mL of CH2Cl2/CH3OH (v/v = 3/2) with the subsequent addition of N,N-dimethylhexadecylamine (0.44 mL, 1.3 mmol). The mixture was stirred at room temperature for 14 h. After the reaction was completed, the reaction solution was concentrated to 3 mL. The residue was poured into 100 mL absolute diethyl ether under stirring and then filtered. The precipitate was filtered, washed with absolute diethyl ether and dried to give compound 1 (0.398 g, yield 90%) as a white solid. 1H NMR (400 MHz, CDCl3, TMS, ppm): δ 0.86–0.90 (t, 3H), 1.23–1.31 (m, 26H), 1.78 (m, 2H), 3.28–3.30 (s, 6H), 3.50–3.55 (m, 2H), 5.20–5.23 (s, 2H), 7.54–7.57 (d, J = 8.4 Hz, 2H), 7.60–7.63 (d, J = 8.4 Hz, 2H). 13C NMR (100 MHz, CDCl3, TMS, ppm): δ 14.11, 22.68, 22.90, 26.31, 29.25, 29.35, 29.39, 29.44, 29.58, 29.65, 29.68, 31.91, 49.45, 63.80, 66.40, 125.60, 126.36, 132.45, 134.89. HRMS (m/z): 438.27 [M + H]+ (calcd for C25H45NBr: 438.2687).

Deionized water (10 mL) was added with syringes to a mixture of compound 1 (0.45 g, 1 mmol), Na2CO3 (0.5 g, 4.7 mmol), Pd(PPh3)4 (200 mg, 0.17 mmol), and thiophene-3-boronic acid (0.128 g, 1 mmol) in EtOH (20 mL) under nitrogen gas flow. After refluxing at 85 °C for 6 h, EtOH was removed under reduced pressure. The residue was extracted with CH2Cl2 (3 × 20 mL) and the resulting organic layer was collected and dried with anhydrous MgSO4. After removal of the solvent under reduced pressure, the residue was purified by column chromatography (eluent: CH2Cl2/CH3OH = 12:1) to give compound 2 (0.2 g, yield 45%) as a colorless solid. 1H NMR (400 MHz, CDCl3, TMS, ppm): δ 0.86–0.90 (t, 3H), 1.23–1.33 (m, 26H), 1.80 (m, 2H), 3.32 (s, 6H), 3.53–3.58 (m, 2H), 5.17 (s, 2H), 7.33–7.36 (m, 1H), 7.38–7.41 (m, 1H), 7.47–7.49 (m, 1H), 7.59–7.62 (d, J = 8 Hz, 2H), 7.70–7.73 (d, J = 8 Hz, 2H). 13C NMR (100 MHz, CDCl3, TMS, ppm): δ 14.12, 22.68, 22.95, 26.34, 29.28, 29.35, 29.40, 29.46, 29.58, 29.65, 29.69, 31.91, 49.55, 63.70, 67.10, 121.64, 125.88, 126.00, 126.79, 126.88, 133.81, 137.98, 140.73. HRMS (m/z): 442.3506 [M + H]+ (calcd for C29H48NS: 442.3500).

The P3ATs were prepared via an oxidative polymerization under nitrogen with the help of FeCl3. 4 equiv of FeCl3 was dissolved in 30 mL of dry CHCl3 under nitrogen, and then 1 equiv of P3ATs dissolved in 20 mL of CHCl3 was added slowly. The mixture was stirred at room temperature for 2 days and 40 mL methanol was added. The resulting precipitate was collected by centrifugation at 3000 rpm for 5 min, washed five times with methanol, and finally dried under vacuum to give the desired P3ATs as a dark red solid. PAT16 (yield: 50.0%). 1H NMR (400 MHz, CD3CN-D2O (v/v = 1/1), TMS, ppm) δ 1.14 (s, br), 1.52 (s, br), 2.06 (s, br), 2.39–2.42 (br), 3.26 (s, br), 3.47 (s, br), 4.94 (s, br), 7.12 (s, br), 7.75–7.8 (dbr), 7.90–7.94 (dbr).

Hydrothermal treatment was used to provide P3ATs the conditions to crystallize into GQDs. Concretely, 30 mg of PT2 was dispersed in 40 mL of NaOH solution (0.5 mM). The NaOH served as an activating agent to loose the tightly packed polythiophene precursors and increase the surface area. The dispersion was ultrasonicated for 10 min and then transferred into an autoclave and heated at 170 °C for a period of 20 h. After cooling to room temperature, the precipitated insoluble large fragments were filtered out using a 0.22 μm membrane and the desired GQDs are present in the supernatant. And then the supernatant was dialysed by 500 Da molecular weight cut-off dialysis bag several times to remove the residual NaOH and small by-products. The resulting GQDs were dispersed in water for further characterization and use.

The redox properties of samples were examined by cyclic voltammetry using a standard three-electrode system, consisting of a platinum sheet as the working electrode, a platinum wire as the counter electrode, and Ag/AgCl as the reference electrode. The electrolyte solution was 0.1 M (Bu)4NBF4 in anhydrous acetonitrile.

Fluorescence imaging of cultured bone marrow-derived (C57BL/6 mice) dendritic cells (DCs) and human adipose-derived stem cells (hADSCs) were performed with a LSM 780 (Zeiss) confocal laser scanning microscope using the light source at 473 nm for excitation and captured in the range of 650 nm-750 nm. Freshly diluted DCs and hADSCs were cultured in media in the presence of GQDs with 50 μg/mL for 30 min at 37 °C. The DCs were collected by centrifugation at 1000 rpm for 1 min, rinsed with PBS (pH 7.4) and repeated three times, then resuspended in PBS solution (2 mL). The DCs suspensions were dropped into a cover glass for fluorescence imaging. The adherent hADSCs were also washed three times with PBS (pH 7.4) for fluorescence imaging.

Hela cells were seeded at a density of 5*103 cells per well in 96-well plates in DMEM medium and incubated for 24 h. The medium was then replaced with 200 μL of medium containing various equivalent concentrations of GQDs and control agents. The cells were incubated for 24 h and cytotoxicity assays were performed using CCK-8 Kits (Dojindo Molecular Technologies, Tokyo, Japan). Absorbance was detected at 450 nm using a TECAN Infinite M200 microplate reader (Tecan, Durham, USA). All data were presented as mean percentages ± standard error of measurement (SEM) in triplicate compared to the optical density values of untreated cells.

For the sake of convenient analyzation, the detailed study was mainly focused on the P3ATs whose R moiety consist of a phenyl ring and alkaneC16 (abbreviated as PAT16). To investigate the evolution process of PAT16, UV–vis absorption and PL spectra were firstly performed. Fig. 1 a describes the UV–vis absorption spectra of PAT16 at different hydrothermal temperatures for 12 h (untreated, 120 °C, 150 °C, 170 °C, respectively). As shown in Fig. S1, the absorption of compound 1 centres at 260 nm and the absorption of compound 2 is also below 320 nm. So in Fig. 1a, the absorption band at 270 nm can be ascribed to the system of phenyl ring and alkaneC16. And the emerging absorption band appearing around 460 nm after compound 2 polymerizing is related to the π→π* electron transition of the conjugate chain in the polymer [23,25,31,32]. As temperature increased to 150 °C, the absorption band around 270 nm and the long wave absorption limit almost remained unchanged, which indicated that the conformation had no marked changes. When hydrothermal temperature increased to 170 °C, the characteristic absorption band centred at 270 nm gradually evolved into a weak shoulder which indicated the configuration of R moiety had changed severely. In the longer wavelength, the maximum absorption wavelength and long wave absorption limit shifted from 450 nm to 461 nm and from 600 nm to 630 nm, respectively. This red-shifted absorption could be ascribed to a partially planar conformation change of the polythiophene chain which possessed a flexible conjugate backbone. The phenomena here may result from the reduced Eg. Because Eg was a crucial parameter concerning the extent of effective conjugation of the products [25]. So, the cyclic voltammetry (Fig. S3) was used to further verify the change of the Eg. The ferrocene/ferrocium (Fc/Fc+) couple was used as a standard (the HOMO energy level was taken to be −4.8 eV). Taking the products derived from different hydrothermal temperature in 12h, the first oxidation peak Eox of samples (under untreated, 120 °C, 150 °C, 170 °C) were measured to be 1.210 eV, 1.183 eV, 0.940 eV, 0.748 eV, respectively, and that of ferrocene was 0.535 eV. For the first reduction peak Ered, they were −0.891 eV, −0.847 eV, −0.787 eV, −0.571 eV, respectively, and ferrocene was 0.435 eV. Using formula [33] (HOMO = − (Eox – E(Fc/Fc+) + 4.8) eV, LUMO = − (Ered – E(Fc+/Fc) + 4.8) eV), the values and Eg were given in Table .1 . These results demonstrate that with the temperature increase the LUMO energy level becomes lower, and the HOMO energy level becomes higher. The Eg did decrease as the temperature increases, which was consistent with the trend of absorption spectrum and PL spectrum (Fig. S2). The analysis of valence band by Ultraviolet Photoelectron Spectrometer (UPS) shows the same trend (Fig. S4). Combining related theory of conjugated polymers with this phenomenon, we speculated that some higher level arrangements with an effective extended π-electron conjugation system had formed [34–40]. As the HRTEM image (Fig. S5a) shows, the extended conjugate structure began to emerge, which corresponds to the above spectral analysis.

Then, we set the reaction temperature fixed at 170 °C to investigate the evolution process with the change of reaction duration (8 h, 12 h, 16 h, 20 h). Just like the phenomena at fixed reaction time with varied temperature, here (Fig. 1b), the whole absorption curve appeared red shift and the long wave absorption limit under 170 °C/20 h condition extended to ∼700 nm, which signified the higher conjugate level. The PL spectra (Fig. 1c) also confirmed the result, where the center peak position had shifted from ∼570 nm to ∼700 nm. In order to have a deep insight into this evolution process, transmission electron microscopy (TEM) and Raman spectra was carried out to record the morphology and structural feature of the products.

The PAT16 was of homodisperse and appeared to be some cross-linking state (Fig. S6a). Hydrothermal process (at 170 °C for 8 h) made the state of PAT16 more flexible and relaxed the conjugated backbone (Fig. S6b). In this stage, from the infrared spectroscopy (IR) (Fig. 4), we took heed of the newly emerging peaks at 1040 cm−1 (external bending vibrations of unsaturated CH) which means the hydrocarbon groups of PAT16 had suffered from the elimination of hydrogen. And another emerging peak at 1694 cm−1 (CO) indicated that the oxygen from the hydrothermal environment began to erode the polymer. It should be pointed out that the characteristic peak of thiophene ring at ∼817 cm−1 and ∼719 cm−1 [41–43] just had small changes, which indicated the thiophene skeleton was less damaged. Further hydrothermal reaction (at 170 °C for 12 h) resulted in the separating out of some small independent particles from the raw material (Fig. S6c). When prolong reaction time to 16h, much more independent particles separated out (Fig. S6d). From the Raman spectra (Fig. 2 a), there are two characteristic peaks centred at ca. 1450 cm−1 and 1345 cm−1, corresponding to the presence of CC stretching mode and distorted D band (sp3 defects). In the field of research on polythiophene, the peak strength centred at ca. 1450 cm−1 is used to reflect the conjugate degree of the polymer [41,44–47]. Because our research objects were evolved from polythiophene and its structural basis was still a conjugated thiophene skeleton. So the peak strength of CC stretching mode still reflects the conjugate degree of the product. The relatively intensity of D band and CC stretching mode expresses the atomic ratio of sp3/sp2, which represents a degree of disorder about the materials. The I D/I C
C stretching mode ratios of PAT16-170 °C-8 h, PAT16-170 °C-12 h, PAT16-170 °C-16 h, and PAT16-170 °C-20 h are 0.69, 0.62, 0.57 and 0.48, respectively. This evolution process suggesting that the conjugate levels were gradually increased as the hydrothermal duration extended. According to the spectral data and the morphology, we consider that the sample might undergo a self-assembly process based the original conjugate structure with the activation of NaOH in hydrothermal condition. In the meantime, Raman spectra also supported the conclusion that the extended π-electron conjugation system was formed corresponding to the red shift of the absorption spectrum. So we combined the changes in IR and speculated that the extended conjugated structure was generated from the R moiety by elimination of hydrogen and its aromatic carbonization to the original conjugated structures.

In order to deeply understand the separating out of those particles, the structure of PAT16 should be studied. Unlike the electrochemical synthesis, the PAT16 synthesized by FeCl3 oxidative polymerization contains a significant amount of regiochemical defects, as shown in Scheme 1. This regiochemical defects partly derived from the interactions among side substituents [48–50]. The irregular head-to-head (HH) or tail-to-tail (TT) linkages were detected as 25% in a similar study by McCullough et al. [51]. It is highly probable that the (TT) position, with weaker and less interaction, would be the cleft as the hydrothermal treating goes on. While the (HH) and head-to-tail (HT) sections would continue the evolution to extended conjugate structure based on the polythiophene framework in conjugate plane. By a certain ratio of irregular linkages, it was easy to imagine so many dots separating out from the raw materials.

When raised the temperature and prolonged the reaction time based on 170 °C for 20 h, we couldn't obtain uniform colloidal dispersion, just a lot of precipitation. From the fluorescence property and morphology we care about, the sample derived from 170 °C for 20 h was the optimal. So a series of characterizations as follows were further carried out and confirmed those monodisperse dots were graphene quantum dots (GQDs). The obtained GQDs solution exhibited high stability and was stored at 4 °C for 6 months without any noticeable precipitation. The size distribution were found to agree well with the Gaussian distribution (Fig. 3 a and inset), and the average size is 3.1 nm by statistical analysis of more than one hundred particles. The full-width-at-half-maximum (FWHM) value of the distribution curve is 0.95 nm, showing a good monodispersed property. The crystalline structure of the GQDs was certified by the selected area electron diffraction (SAED) image as shown in Fig. 3b. The bright spot pattern clearly indicates crystallinity and regular order among the constituents of the GQDs. The marked lines correspond to (100 facet) and (004 facet) with the d approximately equaling to 0.255 nm and 0.174 nm [13]. The HRTEM image in Fig. 3c and the line-profile in Fig. 3d show the lattice spacing of about 0.25 nm which agrees with the result of SAED image (100 facet). The HRTEM images shown in Fig. S5a and S5b, present the resulting morphologies at 170 °C for 12 h and 20 h. According to the pictures, we can know that the duration of the hydrothermal treatment (170 °C) is very important for organizing conjugated structure.

Further evidence for the crystallinity is provided by XRD. The XRD pattern of the GQDs exhibit a broad peak (002 facet) centred at around 22.5° corresponding to interlayer spacing of 0.39 nm (Fig. 5a), which is greater than that of graphite structure. This can be attributed to the presence of functional groups at the edges of the GQDs, which will be further discussed below. Beyond that, another sharp diffraction peak appeared at around 45° corresponding to lattice planes with a spacing of 0.201 nm (100 facet). Together with the result from SAED and HRTEM, there are a few differences among these methods. And the as-prepared GQDs also differ at some degree from the perfect graphene in aspect of (100) facet lattice spacing (0.213 nm), which suggests the obtained GQDs have some lattice distortions in graphite phase. This phenomenon is because of the intervention of N, S atoms and some functional groups in intra-plane, which will be defined in following characterizations. The Si peak at around 31.7° arises from the Si substrate used in the measurement [52].

Carrying the above questions, the XPS data of the prepared GQDs samples were measured for analysis of element and chemical configuration. The full scan XPS spectrum of the GQDs is shown in Fig. 5b. Four peaks at 284.64eV, 163.98eV, 402.32eV and 531.97eV correspond to C1s, S2p, N1s and O1s, respectively [42,53–55]. The bulk element analytical results of XPS indicate that the ratios of the four elements are C 82.89, S 3.57, N 1.83 and O 11.71 wt%, which suggests the introduction of oxygen from the hydrothermal environment. The high resolution spectrum of C1s (Fig. 5c) can be fitted into three main wide peaks centred at 284.5, 285.2 and 287.7 eV, which are attributed to graphitic structure (sp2 CC), CN/CS, and CO bonds, respectively. The high resolution spectrum of S2p (Fig. 5f) exhibits three main peaks. The binding energy peaks at 163.97 eV and 165.1 eV are 2p3/2 and 2p1/2, respectively, of thiophene-S due to spin–orbit coupling. The peak at 168.0 eV is the oxide-S which present another state of sulfur eroded by oxygen. The high resolution spectra of N1s (Fig. 5d) reveal the presence of pyrrolic type N (400 eV), NH groups N (402.3 eV) and NO2 bonds (406 eV) and the first implies that part of N atoms are bonded to the C atoms of GQDs, with the form of five-member ring structure. The high resolution spectra of O1s (Fig. 5e) reveal the presence of COC/COH (531.2 eV), CO (532.1 eV) and the oxygen physically adsorbed on the surface of GQDs (533.2 eV). Fourier transform infrared (FTIR) spectra (Fig. 4 ) further confirm the analysis of XPS. The bands at about 3100–3600 cm−1 originate from the stretching vibrations of OH and NH. The absorption peaks at ∼3025 cm−1, ∼2919 cm−1 and 2849 cm−1 are ascribed to stretching vibrations of CH in aromatic rings (sp2 C), the asymmetric and symmetric stretching of CH (sp3 C), respectively. The newly emerging peaks of the GQDs at ∼1695 cm−1 and 1208 cm−1 are assigned to conjugated carbonyl functional groups CO and the external bending vibrations of unsaturated CH. The peaks at ∼1603 cm−1, ∼1370 cm−1 and ∼1208 cm−1 originate from the skeletal vibration of aromatic rings, stretching vibrations of CN and COH. And the absorption at 1455 cm−1 is due to the superposition of deformation vibration of hydrocarbon and the CC skeleton vibration division in the aromatic ring. In addition, the week absorption at ∼817 cm−1 and ∼719 cm−1 are ascribed to the characteristic absorption peak of thiophene ring [41–43]. The atomic force microscopy (AFM) image (Fig. S7) shows the topographic height of the GQDs assembled on the mica substrate. In order to avoid excessive concentrations, we select the less dense areas (marked line) with a typical topographic height of 1.5–2 nm, which indicates about 3 atomic layer thickness.

Based on the morphologies and structure analysis, we further confirm the fact that the GQDs were mainly conjugated structures with a certain amount of N, S elements conjugated to the carbon matrix and other functional groups connected to the edges and surfaces. By the analysis of structures and photophysical property about GQDs (or CQDs), many hypotheses, namely emissive traps [56–60], electronic conjugate structures [17,61–65], the molecules states and edge states have been proposed [66–72]. Dong et al. [54] believed that the photophysical properties of CQDs should be attributed to the radiative recombination of electrons and holes trapped on the CQDs surface state caused by doped nitrogen and sulfur atoms. And the introduced sulfur atoms would enhance the effect of nitrogen atoms on the properties of the doped carbon nanomaterials through a cooperative effect. Li and co-authors present that the sulfur introduces additional energy levels between π and π* of carbon [42], which provides one more electron transition pathways for both absorption and PL emission. So it should be noted that the excited electrons may experience interband relaxation and then recombination with holes.

It can be seen from absorption spectra (Fig. 1a and b) that the graphene quantum dots basically inherited the absorption characteristics of the precursor except the spectral red shift. And the spectral red shift was just due to the extended π-electron conjugation which had been proved by Raman spectra. The electronic structure of polythiophene depends mainly on the existence of conjugate thiophene. The existence of thiophene structure in GQDs has been confirmed by IR and XPS (Figs. 4 and 5 f). Therefore, the sulfur related electron energy level must exist in the GQDs and plays a decisive role in internal electron transition behaviour. Similarly, the excitation independent behaviour have also been studied by Yang et al. and Han et al. which are determined by doped nitrogen atoms [17] and fluorine atoms [73], respectively. As for our experimental results above, the excitation independent (Fig. S8) PL behaviour, not with the same size GQDs, can be ascribed to internal structure rather than size factor. For the purpose of understanding the panorama of the prepared conjugate system (GQDs), it is necessary to define the optical properties in a concrete way. The UV–vis absorption, photoluminescence (PL) and excitation spectra were acquired from the aqueous dispersion of GQDs and shown in Fig. 1d. By referencing to other reported CQDs [74,75], it is reasonable to ascribe the absorption shoulder (evolved from carbon structure) in ∼270 nm to the π→π* transition of the aromatic sp2 domains. The longer wavelength absorption bands at ∼466 nm were evolved from the absorption of conjugate polythiophene and had never present in no sulfur doping GQDs. Neither did appear the PL maximum reaching to 700 nm in the case of no sulfur doping GQDs. As for the PL shoulder above 700 nm, it can be ascribed to the capture state served by oxygen-containing functional groups (the oxide-S, carbonyl group) and other groups, which had been certified by XPS and IR.

To further clarify the electron transfer process and photophysical properties of GQDs, the time-resolved PL spectra and PL quantum yield (QY) were measured. PL decays of GQDs under different excitation wavelengths were shown in Fig. 6a–6c. The decay curve of GQDs recorded at 700 nm with an excitation of 270 nm shows a three exponential function (τ1 = 0.49 ns, 72.61%. τ2 = 1.96 ns, 26.01%. τ3 = 10.97 ns, 1.38%), suggesting that the PL of the obtained GQDs might originate from three different electron transition pathways among the additional energy levels between π and π* of carbon. When excited by 300 nm and 320 nm at same recorded wavelength, the curves could be well fitted to a two-exponential model (τ1 = 0.36 ns, 89.03%. τ2 = 3.29 ns, 10.97% and τ1 = 0.90 ns, 90.88%. τ2 = 3.63 ns, 9.12%, respectively). The absence of the slowest decay (above 10 ns) means that the electron migration paths become simpler with the excitation of longer wavelengths [43,64,76,77].

Fluorescence emission is a random process, and the excited electrons are bound to pass through the doped energy levels in different paths. In this work, the doped nitrogen and sulfur levels play an important role in achieving low photon energy emission. The decay processes responsible for returning to the ground state mainly include fluorescence emission and non-radiation relaxation. When excited by high energy photon, the excited electrons experience multiple non-radiative relaxation to achieve the radiation of low photon energy. In the meantime, the capture states at higher energy levels also have significant energy losses. Therefore, there's a lot of energy dissipation as a result of a large rate of internal conversion and have a small rate of emission [52]. As the energy of the excited photon decreasing, electrons cannot be excited to higher energy levels and the redundant electron relaxation process is simplified. The PL quantum yield (QY) of the GQDs was measured to be 1.28%, 1.67% and 2.06% by the absolute QY measurement method using excitation of 320 nm, 340 nm and 462 nm, respectively. When excitation wavelength shifted to longer wavelength at 530 nm, the QY reached to 3.92%, which meant energy dissipation during internal conversion was somewhat eliminated and the quantum yield was improved by the avoidance of some energy loss. The current low QY of GQDs in monodisperse states might be attributed to the strong competitions between π-electron confinement within the conjugate structure and delocalization by the capture state mentioned above, which greatly increase energy dissipation [76].

So combining conjugate structures mainly composed of C, S, N atoms with the optical properties, we proposed a tentative mechanism mainly led by electronic conjugate structures to explain the long wavelength emission [42,43]. The conjugated effect of nitrogen and sulfur introduces two new kind of intermediate energy levels (Sπ*, Nπ*) between π and π* of carbon. When excited by short wavelength (below 260 nm) (Fig. S9), two emission peaks at ∼355 nm and ∼700 nm appeared in the PL spectra with same order of magnitude, which indicate there exist intermediate levels as mentioned above. So ultimately, the radiative transition will take place on Nπ* and Sπ* intermediate energy levels corresponding to the emission at ∼355 nm and ∼700 nm, respectively. But when the wavelength of excitation is between 300 nm and 600 nm, the radiative transition will take place mainly on Sπ* with excitation-independent emission at ∼700 nm. The possible PL processes of GQDs were schematically shown in Fig. 7 . Furthermore, the GQDs exhibited good photostability continuously irradiated by a 450W xenon lamp. As shown in Fig. 8 , the PL of the prepared GQDs (emission at 700 nm was checked) exhibited a good stability with above 97% of initial intensity after 60 min irradiation, which is comparable to CdSe/ZnS core-shell QDs (conventional red-emitting semiconductor QDs) [78].

In the end, we find that the length of the alkyl chain in poly(3-alkylthiophene) has an important effect on the final optical properties. The P3ATs whose R moiety consists of a phenyl ring and alkaneC14/C12 (abbreviated as PAT14, PAT12) were also investigated in same hydrothermal treating conditions (at 170 °C for 20 h). The UV–vis absorption spectra of final products shown in Fig. S10 move to longer wavelengths with the elongation of the length of alkyl side chain. The PL spectra keep in accordance with absorption spectra, and the PL maximum emission are 664 nm, 688 nm and 700 nm corresponding to the products derived from PAT12, PAT14 and PAT16, respectively (Fig. S11). The GQDs derived from PAT12, PAT14 and PAT16 were also characterized by Raman spectra (Fig. 2b). The I D/I C
C stretching mode ratios of GQDs derived from PAT12, PAT14 and PAT16 are 0.58, 0.52 and 0.48, respectively. This phenomenon revealed that the longer the side chain, the higher the conjugation extent of the reaction products, which is consistent with the phenomenon of red shift spectra we observed (Figs. S10 and S11). The relationship between the optical properties and the degree of conjugation is same as we discussed earlier. The TEM pictures for samples evolved from PAT12 and PAT14 are shown in Fig. S12a and S12b. With the puzzle about PL differences, we restudy the conformation of P3ATs.

As shown in Scheme 1 and Scheme 2 , any defect in the ideal head-to-tail (HT) relative placement of consecutive monomer units induces a severe steric repulsion between substituents, and the existence of essentially single bonds between the aromatic cycles allows the occurrence of interannular rotations, which bring about a loss of effective conjugation [25]. But For the hydrothermal treatment we used here, the high importance is the disruption of the close packed polymer chains [21]. The small distance and the strong interaction between polymer chains can reduce the solubility of the polymer, which means more resistance to extend the conjugated structure based on polythiophene frame. While the disruption of the close packing could relax the conjugated backbone and increased the contact area to hydrothermal environment, which benefited for active atoms to self-assemble into existing conjugate structure along the conjugate direction. Winokur et al. [79] found that this disruption can be enhanced with the elongation of the P3ATs alkyl chain length. Although the degree of conjugation is reduced along its length direction by severe steric repulsion among longer alkyl chain, the disruption would provide more sites to extend the conjugate planes in the direction perpendicular to the chain. Taken together, longer alkyl chain is easier to form particles with high degree of monodisperse conjugation. So, the regulation on PL of final products can carried out by controlling the alkyl chain length of P3ATs.

Based on the highly photostability (Fig. 8), water solubility and red-emitting of GQDs derived from PAT16, we attempted to apply this GQDs as fluorescence imaging agents. So CCK-8 Kits were carried out to evaluate the cytotoxicity of the GQDs. The cell viabilities of Hela cells were tested after being exposed to GQDs at different concentrations (Fig. S13). As observed, the cell viabilities of Hela cells maintained a high level upon addition of the GQDs at up to 100 μg mL−1, which suggests that the GQDs have low cytotoxicity. Importantly, considering the concentrations typically used in bioimaging applications (<100 μg/mL), the cytotoxicity of the GQDs was found to be acceptable. After demonstrating the lesser cytotoxicity characteristics of the GQDs, we then explored the feasibility of GQDs for live cell imaging. For facilitating the follow-up study, the cultured bone marrow-derived (C57BL/6 mice) dendritic cells (DCs) and human adipose-derived stem cells were selected for tests. A remarkable intracellular fluorescence was observed in the confocal laser scanning microscopy image (Fig. 9 ). The bright-field and overlapped fluorescence images reveal that the fluorescence signals are from the perinuclear regions of the cytosol, thus indicating good cell-permeability of the GQDs into living cells.

In summary, the conversion of P3ATs into GQDs was presented. The successive thermal conversion process actually comprises three steps. Firstly, the agminated P3ATs relax to loose structure to increase the contact area with hydrothermal environment. Secondly, volatile moieties like alkyl chains turn into fusion state with cyclodehydrogenation and nano-conjugate phase separates from matrixs, which caused by the structural characteristics of P3ATs mentioned above. And finally, the separated parts evolve into extended conjugation cores with uniform size. The successful conversion to GQDs make the maximum emission peak extended to 700 nm. And the emission wavelength can be modulated by changing the alkyl chain length of P3ATs within certain limits. Based on this strategy, a variety of GQDs with specific doping element could be synthesized, probably also from organic molecules other than P3ATs. This new pathway allows the preparation of GQDs with molecularly designed chemical structures that can be considered fusion materials of organic-based frameworks and carbon materials.
There are no conflicts of interest to declare.

ce:acknowledgment.ce:para: This work was supported by National Science Foundation of Shandong Province (ZR2016EMM20, ZR2016EMQ09), Shenzhen Science and Technology Research and Development Funds (JCYJ20160331173823401) and the National Natural Science Foundations of China (51472150). The authors deeply acknowledge Prof. Huaijin Zhang, Haohai Yu, Xiaoqiang Yu and Qi Fang for their support in this project. The authors thank Dr. Shouzhi Wang and Yingying Jin for assistance with cyclic voltammetry characterization.

ce:section.ce:para: Supplementary data to this article can be found online at https://doi.org/10.1016/j.carbon.2018.10.047.
